C002.claim: Imagine a prism splitting sunlight into a rainbow—this happens because blue light slows down more than red light in glass, making them bend at different angles.
C002.query: Dispersion Imagine a prism splitting sunlight into a rainbow—this happens because blue light slows down more than physics lecture notes textbook explanation
C002.result_snippet: Dispersion of light is the phenomenon in which white light splits into its constituent colors when it passes through a prism. This happens because each color of light has a different wavelength and bends by a different amount upon refraction. As a result, the colors spread out to form a visible spectrum, commonly known as VIBGYOR (Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red). Dispersion ... Explore dispersion of light: how prisms & water split white light into vibrant colors of the rainbow. Understand wavelengths, rainbows & spectroscopy. Spectroscopy is one major advancement in physics which totally relies on the theory of dispersion whereas the mesmerizing view of rainbows is the natural art of dispersion. While refraction also means the bending of light, dispersion slightly differs by including the aspect of separation of white light into colors based on wavelength. To understand why dispersion occurs, we must recall that white light is a mixture of light of different wavelengths. Each colour in white light corresponds to a particular wavelength. When light passes from air into glass, its speed changes. Since glass is optically denser than air, light slows down upon entering the prism. However, all wavelengths do not slow down by the same amount. The ...
